supporting_evidence: Clear history of fall at home consistent with simple mechanical fall
81-year-old female — falls are the leading cause of injury in this population
Multiple fall risk factors: age, osteoporosis, polypharmacy (warfarin, enalapril, supplements)
Vital signs stable with no signs of hemodynamic compromise
Hip pain after fall consistent with muscular or soft tissue contusion
Head strike likely caused scalp contusion
Dizziness may be related to pain and anxiety response
Most common outcome of falls in the elderly
Patient on warfarin anticoagulation with head trauma
Anticoagulation is a well-established risk factor for traumatic intracranial bleeding
Reported dizziness may indicate early neurological involvement
CT head should be performed urgently
Hip pain after fall in patient with known osteoporosis
Hip fracture is common in elderly women after falls
May require X-ray or MRI for definitive diagnosis
Dizziness reported — may have preceded the fall
Atrial fibrillation can cause transient cerebral hypoperfusion
ECG and orthostatic vital signs recommended

reasoning: 81-year-old female presenting after fall at home with head strike and hip pain. Mechanical fall is the most likely diagnosis given the history and demographics. Soft tissue injury is expected given the mechanism. Intracranial hemorrhage is flagged as the critical don't-miss diagnosis due to warfarin anticoagulation combined with head trauma — urgent CT is required. Hip fracture must be excluded given osteoporosis. Syncope should be investigated as a potential precipitating cause of the fall, particularly given the known atrial fibrillation.
